chunk_summary: This section discusses how monitoring and evaluation (M&E) metrics in pediatric nutrition programs can transition from dashboard signals to actionable diagnoses and corrective plans, focusing on common signals in Indian nutrition programs.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of M&E metrics in identifying and addressing pediatric nutrition issues.
Learn to interpret dashboard signals and perform differential diagnoses for corrective actions.
Develop strategies to improve data quality and program effectiveness in community nutrition settings.
key_facts: Signal 1: Sharp drop in wasting prevalence may indicate measurement errors, selective reporting, or true improvement; corrective actions include sentinel re-measurement and calibration audits.

Signal 2: High adolescent anemia rates despite PM POSHAN meals may result from low dietary iron density, poor WIFS adherence, or helminth burden; corrective actions include plate-waste observation and deworming coverage checks.

Signal 3: Near 100% growth monitoring coverage may still miss severe wasting due to poor anthropometry techniques or referral delays; bedside anthropometry and referral handoff strengthening are critical.
PM POSHAN meals are reported as served on most days, but anemia rates remain high, requiring multi-causal analysis beyond meal provision.
Corrective actions emphasize sentinel re-measurement, menu composition reviews, and strengthening referral systems for high-risk children.
reference_quote: Guidelines on PM POSHAN Scheme emphasize the importance of fortified inputs and supervised dosing registers to address adolescent anemia rates effectively. (Reference 2)
document_key_topics: Monitoring and Evaluation Metrics in Pediatric Nutrition
Dashboard Signal Interpretation and Corrective Actions
